pages.text: Effects of calcination temperature of kaolinite clays on the properties of geopolymer cements A. Elimbi ⇑, H.K. Tchakoute, D. Njopwouo Laboratoire de Physico chimie des Matériaux Minéraux, Faculté des Sciences, B.P. 812 Yaoundé, Cameroon article info Article history: Received 2 August 2010 Received in revised form 22 December 2010 Accepted 24 December 2010 Available online 19 January 2011 Keywords: Kaolinite Calcination Alkaline solution Geopolymer cement Setting time Linear shrinkage Compressive strength abstract The aim of this work is to determine the most convenient calcination temperature of kaolinite clays in view of producing geopolymer cements. In this light, the clay fractions of three kaolin minerals were used. The clay fractions were characterized (chemical and thermal analyses and X-ray diffraction) and then calcined in the temperature range of 450 and 800 /C176C. The obtained amorphous materials were dis- solved in a strongly alkaline solution in order to produce geopolymer cements whose pastes were char- acterized by determining their setting time, linear shrinkage and compressive strength. Hardened geopolymer cement paste samples were also submitted to X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and scanning electron microscopy analyses. The setting time of geopolymer cement pastes produced from the clay fractions calcined at 450 /C176C was very long (test samples could be handled easily only after 21 days at the ambient atmosphere of the laboratory). For the clay fractions calcined between 500 and 700 /C176C, the setting time of geopolymer cement pastes reduced with increasing temperature and varied between 130 and 40 min. Above 700 /C176C, the setting time began to increase. The linear shrinkage of the hardened geopolymer cement paste samples aged between 21 and 28 days attained its lowest value around 700 /C176C. Above 700 /C176C, the linear shrinkage began to increase. The compressive strength of the hardened geopolymer cement paste samples was between 11.9 and 36.4 MPa: it increased with samples from the clay fractions calcined between 500 and 700 /C176C but dropped above 700 /C176C. It can be concluded that the most convenient temperature for the calcination of kaolinite clays in view of producing geopolymer cements is around 700 /C176C. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Among hydraulic binders common in our modern world, Port- land cements remain the most used [1]. However, production of Portland cement is a resource exhausting, energy intensive process that releases large amounts of the green house gas CO 2 into the atmosphere [2]. Nevertheless, many studies are being carried out to design new hydraulic binders which can not only substitute Portland cements, but can also be produced in a way as to safe- guard our environment. Among these hydraulic binders, geopoly- mer cements present interesting potential [3]. In 1970s Davidovits stated that, geopolymers are a new class of three dimensional silico-aluminate materials [4]. Products arising from geopolymer reactions have certain advantages: high mechan- ical properties, resistance to high temperature and chemical agents, low shrinkage, etc. [3]. The synthesis of geopolymers can be carried out by using the sol–gel method. The raw materials commonly used are those rich in alumino-silicate such as natural pozzolan, ﬂy ash, blast furnace slag, and calcined kaolinite clays. Metakaolin is essentially an anhydrous alumino-silicate

the amorphous materials obtained, geopolymer cement pastes were synthesized and characterized by determination of setting time, linear shrinkage and compressive strength. The hardened geopolymer cement pastes were also subjected to the following analyses: X-rays diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectros- copy and scanning electron microscopy. 2. Materials and experimental methods 2.1. Materials The three clay materials used originate from the Littoral Region of Cameroon. Previous studies done on these clay materials labeled as BO 1,B O 4 and Dib1 respec- tively showed that kaolinite is the major mineral and quartz the major impurity. Taking into account density differences between quartz and kaolinite, these clay materials were enriched with kaolinite using the sedimentometry process based on Stockes’ law [18]. The dried clay fractions were crushed in a mortar and then sieved to 80 lm. The clay fractions were each calcined in a programmable electric furnace (Nabertherm, Mod. LH 60/14) for 10 h at a heating rate of 5 /C176C/min at the following temperatures: 450, 500, 550, 600, 650, 700, 750 and 800 /C176C. The alkaline solution used was a mixture of aqueous solution of sodium hydrox- ide 10 M and sodium silicate with bulk density of 1350 kg/m 3 in a weight ratio of 1:1. The sodium hydroxide solution was obtained by dissolving dried pellets of 99% purity in distilled water. The sodium silicate solution had a composition by weight of 28.7% SiO 2, 8.9% Na 2O and 62.4% H 2O. 2.2. Experimental methods The geopolymer cement pastes consisted of a mixture of alkaline solution with powdered calcined clay fractions of weight ratio 0.68. The assembly was mixed in a Hobart mixer ( M&O , model N50-G ) for 5 min. The pastes obtained were used to determine the setting time and to make geopolymer cement samples. The geopoly- mer cement paste samples were molded in cylindrical PCV moulds (diameter 3.1 cm, high 6.2 cm). Once molded, they were vibrated for 10 min on an electrical vibrating table ( M&O , type 202, N /C176106) to remove entrapped air bubbles. During hardening of the geopolymer cement paste samples, the cylinders were covered with a thin ﬁlm of polyethylene to avoid water evaporation and then kept for 24 h at the ambient atmosphere of the laboratory (24–26 /C176C) before demolding. The geopolymer cement pastes obtained were labeled as PBO 1 (the products from BO1), PBO4 (the products from BO 4) and PDib 1 (the products from Dib 1). Depending on the number of days (1–28 days) the hardened geopolymer cement cylinder paste samples stayed in the ambient atmosphere of the laboratory, the measurements of linear shrinkage and compressive strength were carried out. After measurement of the compressive strength, certain fragments of geopolymer cement paste samples were kept for XRD, FTIR and SEM analyses. The setting time was measured using the Vicat apparatus according to the EN 196-3 standard. The measurement of linear shrinkage was carried out with a vernier calliper on the hardened geopolymer ce- ment cylinder paste samples aged of 1, 7, 14, 21 and 28 days respectively. The chemical analyses of the clay fractions were carried out using ICP-AES technique. The crystalline phases were determined using X-ray diffractometry with the aid of a Bruker D 5000 diffractometer, operating by reﬂexion of k a1 radiation of Cu, according to the following operating conditions: voltage 40 kV, current 40 mA, angular domain scanned 6 /C1766 2h 6 70/C176. The

there is a drop in linear shrinkage: lowest values are obtained around 700 /C176C. When the clay fractions are calcined above 700 /C176C, a beginning of rearrangement of the metakaolinite struc- ture could occur and the linear shrinkage of the samples begins to increase. The weaker values of linear shrinkage of PDib 1, com- pared to those of PBO 1 or PBO 4 may originate from slightly higher quartz content in Dib 1 material ( Fig. 1 ). 3.2.6. Compressive strength The compressive strength of hardened geopolymer cement cyl- inder paste samples as a function of calcination temperature of the clay fractions is presented in Fig. 6 e. It is shown that compressive strength increases between 500 and 700 /C176C then drops afterwards. These results vary in the same order as those observed with setting time or linear shrinkage. It is evident that when the dehydroxylation of kaolinite clays takes place, the structure of the resulting metakaolinite is in a ‘‘state of disorder’’ which depends on the calcination temperature. This structure attains the maximum disorder around 700 /C176C. In- crease of disorder leads to better characteristics of geopolymer products. Calcination of kaolinite clays above 700 /C176C decreases the tendency to disorder within the structure of metakaolinite, giv- ing rise to less performant geopolymer products. 4. Conclusion Using metakaolinite as an alumino-silicate material for making of geopolymer cements, kaolinite clays were calcined at various temperatures. The determination of certain characteristics (setting time, linear shrinkage and compressive strength) carried out on the geopolymer cement pastes produced from kaolinite clays calcined between 450 and 800 /C176C showed that: – The setting time decreases when calcination is carried out between 500 and 700 /C176C. Above 700 /C176C, there is an increase of setting time. – The linear shrinkage of the geopolymer cement paste samples aged from 21 to 28 days attains its lowest value around 700 /C176C. Above this temperature, the linear shrinkage begins to increase. – The compressive strength of hardened geopolymer cement paste samples increases with increasing the calcination temper- ature of kaolinite clays between 500 and 700 /C176C but drops above 700 /C176C. From these results, it happens that when the dehydroxylation of kaolinite clays takes place, the structure of metakaolinite is in a ‘‘state of disorder’’ which varies with calcination temperature. This structure attains maximum disorder around 700 /C176C. An increase of disorder in metakaolinite promotes better characteristics of geo- polymer products. References [1] Temuujin J, Van Riessen A. Effect of ﬂy ash preliminary calcination on the properties of geopolymer. J Hazard Mater 2009;164(2–3):634–9. [2] Guo X, Shi H, Dick WA. Compressive strength and microstructural characteristics of class C ﬂy ash geopolymer. Cem Concr Compos 2010;32(2):142–7. [3] Yunsheng Z, Wei S, Zongjin L. Composition design and microstructural characterization of calcined kaolin-based geopolymer cement. Appl Clay Sci 2010;47(3–4):271–5. [4] Ciofﬁ R, Maffucci L, Santoro L. Optimization of geopolymer synthesis by calcination and polycondensation of a kaolinitic residue. Resour Conserv Recycl 2003;40(1):27–38. [5] Davidovits J, Orlinski J, editors, In: Proceedings of geopolymer 88, European conference on soft mineralurgy. Université de Technologie, Compiègne, France, vol. 1–2; 1988. [6] Deepak A, Alan C, Russell FH. The transformation of kaoli

For the geopolymer test samples whose age is between 21 and 28 days and whose calcination of clay fractions was done at 700 /C176C, linear shrinkage generally attained the lowest values. Be- fore the formation of the gel phase and the hardening of geopoly- mer materials, metakaolinite particles undergo substantial change during dissolution, restructuring and polycondensation [28].I n particular, linear shrinkage originates from capillary tensions with- in the gel framework during geopolymerization [29]. For the hard- ened geopolymer paste samples obtained from the clay fractions calcined between 500 and 600 /C176C, the increasing of linear shrink- age values when the age of the test samples varies between 14 and 28 days seemed to be linked to the ‘degree of disorder’ within the structure of metakaolinite. It happens as if in the temperature interval within which metakaolinite is formed, the degree of disor- der within its structure rises and attains a maximum around 700 /C176C. When the degree of disorder is low, the reactivity of meta- kaolinite is such that linear shrinkage of the test samples is high. Thus, between 500 and 600 /C176C, the linear shrinkage is high because at these temperatures, metakaolinite is less reactive. Above 600 /C176C, the disorder within metakaolinite structure becomes high and Fig. 6. Characteristics of the geopolymers. (a) Setting time; (b–d): linear shrinkage; (e): compressive strength. A. Elimbi et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 2805–2812 2811

3.2.2. FTIR spectra The FTIR spectra of hardened geopolymer cement pastes ob- tained with the clay fractions calcined at 450, 600 and 800 /C176C are presented in Fig. 4 . For FTIR spectra of the clay fractions calcined at 450 /C176C, it is clearly established that this temperature is not suf- ﬁcient to break the crystalline structure of kaolinite into metakaol- inite. In fact, the characteristic absorption bands of kaolinite are present (3690–3630 cm /C01) [22]. On the other hand, the absorption band around 1460 cm /C01 in the FTIR spectra is attributed to stretch- ing vibrations of C–O bond of sodium carbonate which induces efﬂorescence within geopolymer materials [23,24]. Apart from the previous observations, all the FTIR spectra ( Fig. 4 ) exhibit absorption bands generally present in metakaolin-based geopoly- mers. This notably concerns the absorption bands at 3450– 3480 cm/C01 and 1650 cm /C01 which correspond respectively to stretching and deformation vibrations of O–H and H–O–H groups from the water molecules contained in these materials [23–26] . The absorption bands at 1085 and 750 cm /C01 are respectively attributed to asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of Si–O–Al and Si–O–Si bonding of AlO 4 and SiO4 tetrahedrons of geopolymer [26,27]. The absence of 920 cm /C01 band and the presence of a peak at 698 cm /C01 conﬁrm that for 600 or 800 /C176C, metakaolinite has undergone geopolymerization. 3.2.3. Microstructure Fig. 5 presents the micrographs of geopolymer from the clay fractions calcined at 600 or 800 /C176C. On the micrographs of geopoly- mers synthesized at 600 /C176C( Fig. 5 a, c and d), geopolymer phases are trapped in a whitish phase of sodium carbonate. However, micrographs of geopolymers obtained at 600 /C176C have a sponge-like aspect while those obtained at 800 /C176C have a compact aspect. This shows that geopolymerization is more signiﬁcant in the clay frac- tions calcined at 800 /C176C than those at 600 /C176C. 3.2.4. Setting time The geopolymer cement pastes obtained from the clay fractions calcined at 450 /C176C could be handled easily only after 21 days at ambient atmosphere of the laboratory and they all showed efﬂo- rescence. Their linear shrinkage was very high (which did not allow Fig. 1. X-rays diffractograms of the clay fractions. Fig. 2. Thermal analyses of the clay fractions. (a) Differential thermal analysis; (b) thermo gravimetric analysis. A. Elimbi et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 2805–2812 2807

for taking these data into account in Fig. 6b–d ) and their X-ray dif- fractograms (Fig. 3) and FTIR spectra ( Fig. 4) revealed the presence of kaolinite. It is accordingly clear that upon heating at 450 /C176C, the thermal energy involved for calcination of the clay fractions into metakaolinite is not sufﬁcient. The variation of setting time as a function of temperature of the geopolymer cement paste samples Fig. 3. X-ray diffractograms of the geopolymers. (a) PDib 1; (b) PBO 1 and (c) PBO 4. 2808 A. Elimbi et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 2805–2812

from the clay fractions calcined between 500 and 800 /C176C is pre- sented in Fig. 6 a. It happens that the setting time varies between 130 and 40 min: it decreases when calcination is carried out be- tween 500 and 700 /C176C for PBO 1 and PBO 4, between 500 and 650 /C176C for PDib 1. Above 700 /C176C (PBO 1 and PBO 4) or 650 /C176C (PDib 1), the setting time begins to increase. From these results it appears that a decrease of setting time of geopolymer cement pastes from the clay fractions calcined between 500 and 700 /C176C correlates with the ‘‘degree of amorphization’’ of metakaolinite. In fact, the thermal analysis results ( Fig. 2 ) indicate that the dehydroxylation of kaolinite is progressive between 400 and 620 /C176C and it is well known that this stage corresponds to the formation of Fig. 4. IR spectra of the geopolymers. (a) PDib 1; (b) PBO 1 and (c) PBO 4. A. Elimbi et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 2805–2812 2809

metakaolinite. It therefore happens that during the calcination pro- cess of the clay fractions, ‘‘the state of disorder’’ within the meta- kaolinite structure increases with increasing temperature and a maximum is attained around 700 /C176C. When geopolymerization is taking place, the ‘‘state of disorder’’ within the structure of meta- kaolinite guarantees an increase of its reactivity. Above 650 /C176C (PDib1) or 700 /C176C (PBO 1 and PBO 4), the setting time begins to rise because the ‘‘state of disorder’’ within the structure of metakaoli- nite decreases. 3.2.5. Linear shrinkage The linear shrinkage as a function of age of the hardened geo- polymer cement pastes produced from the clay fractions calcined between 500 and 800 /C176C and kept at the ambient temperature of the laboratory (24–26 /C176C) is presented in Fig. 6b–d . The overall re- sult indicates that the linear shrinkage is low (0–0.37%). From these data, high values of linear shrinkage are recorded with the test samples from the clay fractions calcined either between 500 and 650 /C176C or above 700 /C176C and aged between 21 and 28 days. Fig. 5. Micrographs of the geopolymers. PBO 1: (a and b); PBO 4: (c and d); PDib 1: (e and f). 2810 A. Elimbi et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 2805–2812
